title: Polymer clay lentil bead set
subtitle: A set of ten flat disc beads with impressed surface texture
excerpt: A set of ten lentil-shaped beads in polymer clay, pressed with a texture stamp and strung as a necklace or bracelet. Lentil beads are flat discs with a hole through the diameter; the shape shows off impressed surface texture clearly. No kiln required. Finished beads approximately 20 mm diameter.
sourceNotes: Lentil bead technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that cures hard in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.

content.text: A lentil bead is a flattened disc with a hole drilled from the edge through the diameter rather than through the face. When strung, the bead faces forward and the hole is invisible. The flat surface is ideal for texture stamps and carved designs.
holds fine stamp impressions clearly and hardens in a domestic oven without a kiln. This set of ten beads makes a necklace or a bracelet depending on the finished diameter.

items.name: Polymer clay in one or more colours
Texture stamp or lace
items.substitution: Fabric mesh, sandpaper, or a carved rubber stamp

content.text: Condition the polymer clay and divide into ten equal pieces of about 5 g each. Roll each piece into a ball, then press it between your palms to form a flat disc approximately 20 mm in diameter and 6 mm thick. The edges should be rounded, not sharp. A uniform thickness across all ten beads means consistent finishing.

Press a texture stamp or a small piece of lace firmly onto one face of each disc. Press straight down and lift straight up, without rocking the stamp, for a clean impression. The texture should be visible but not so deep that it weakens the bead. Repeat on the other face if a double-sided textured bead is wanted.

Pierce each bead through the edge (not through the face) with the needle tool. Enter the needle at the centre point of the edge and push it through to the opposite side. The hole runs horizontally through the disc from one edge to the other. Widen the hole gently by rotating the needle tool. A 2 mm diameter hole is enough for most jewellery cord.

Bake all ten beads on a dedicated oven liner at 110-130°C for 20 minutes. Insert toothpicks through the holes before baking to prevent the holes from closing. Remove toothpicks after curing and allow beads to cool fully.

Sand the flat faces with 800-grit sandpaper for a smooth, polished finish. The sanding removes the slight chalky surface film from curing and brings out the texture detail. String with jewellery cord in a pattern of your choice.
Make beads in two graduating sizes for a tapered necklace: 25 mm centre beads tapering to 15 mm edge beads.
After baking, brush a contrasting acrylic paint over the surface. Wipe back the raised areas to leave colour only in the recessed texture.
Make lentil beads in a two-colour marbled clay for a different surface pattern instead of stamped texture.

rows.problem: Beads are not all the same size
rows.cause: Clay pieces were not weighed or measured before rolling
rows.fix: Divide the clay by cutting equal pieces on a scale before rolling. Consistent weights give consistent sizes.

rows.cause: Clay softened around the hole in the oven heat before setting
rows.fix: Insert a toothpick through every hole before placing in the oven. Remove after curing.

rows.cause: Too little pressure when stamping, or stamp placed on slightly warm clay that had already softened
rows.fix: Press stamps on clay that is freshly conditioned but at room temperature. Use firm, even pressure.
